revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:رأس السنة اليابانية]][[az:Yaponiyada Yeni il]][[bcl:Bagong Taon kan Hapon]][[bg:Японска нова година]][[ca:Cap d'any japonès]][[da:Japansk nytår]][[de:Japanisches Neujahrsfest]][[en:Japanese New Year]][[es:Año Nuevo japonés]][[eu:Japoniar urteberri-egun]][[fa:سال نوی ژاپنی]][[fr:Nouvel An japonais]][[hu:Japán újév]][[hy:Ամանորը Ճապոնիայում]][[id:Tahun baru Jepang]][[it:Capodanno giapponese]][[ja:正月]][[ko:쇼가쓰]][[mn:Японы шинэ жил]][[ms:Tahun Baru Jepun]][[pl:Shōgatsu]][[pt:Ano-novo japonês]][[ro:Anul Nou Japonez]][[ru:Новый год в Японии]][[simple:Japanese New Year]][[sr:Нова година у Јапану]][[ta:சப்பானியப் புத்தாண்டு]][[th:ตรุษญี่ปุ่น]][[tr:Japon Yeni Yılı]][[uk:Новий рік у Японії]][[uz:Yaponiyada yangi yil]][[vi:Tết Nhật Bản]][[zh:日本新年]]
{{Short description|Traditional holiday}} {{For|other traditions of celebrating the lunar new year|Lunar New Year}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2013}} {{Infobox holiday |holiday_name = Japanese New Year ({{Lang|ja-latn|Oshōgatsu}}) |type = asian |image = Kadomatsu M1181.jpg |caption = The {{nihongo3|||{{ill|Kadomatsu|en|Kadomatsu|qid=Q2667233|lt=Kadomatsu|1=Kadomatsu}}}} is a traditional decoration for the new year holiday. |official_name = {{nihongo3||正月|1=Japanese New Year|Shōgatsu}} or {{nihongo3|||Oshōgatsu}} |nickname = New year |observedby = Japan |litcolor = |longtype = Cultural |significance = Celebrates the new year |begins = December 31/January 1 |ends = January 4 |scheduling = same day each year |date = January 1 |frequency=annual |duration=1 day |observances = Holiday |relatedto = {{ill|New Year's Day|en|New Year's Day|qid=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day|1=New Year's Day}}, {{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|qid=Q131772|lt=Chinese New Year|1=Chinese New Year}}, {{ill|Korean New Year|en|Korean New Year|qid=Q8249787|lt=Korean New Year|1=Korean New Year}}, {{ill|Tết|en|Tết|qid=Q912988|lt=Vietnamese New Year|1=Tết}}, {{ill|Tsagaan Sar|en|Tsagaan Sar|qid=Q2340640|lt=Mongolian New Year|1=Tsagaan Sar}}, {{ill|Losar|en|Losar|qid=Q1347838|lt=Tibetan New Year|1=Losar}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Japanese New Year'''|正月|1=Japanese New Year|Shōgatsu}} is an annual {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|qid=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}} that takes place in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Since 1873, the official Japanese New Year has been celebrated according to the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}, on January 1 of each year, {{nihongo|{{ill|New Year's Day|en|New Year's Day|qid=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day|1=New Year's Day}}|元日|Ganjitsu}}. Prior to 1872, traditional events of the Japanese New Year were celebrated on the first day of the year on the modern {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, the last official lunisolar calendar.
==History== Prior to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the date of the Japanese New Year had been based on Japanese versions of lunisolar calendar (the last of which was the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}) and, prior to {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|en|Jōkyō calendar|qid=Q2353004|lt=Jōkyō calendar|1=Jōkyō calendar}}, the Chinese version. However, in 1873, five years after the [[Meiji Restoration]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} adopted the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} and the first day of January became the official and cultural New Year's Day in Japan.
==Traditional food== [[File:Oseti.jpg|thumb|right|{{nihongo3|||{{ill|Osechi|en|Osechi|qid=Q862557|lt=Osechi-ryōri|1=Osechi}}}}, typical new year's dishes]] [[File:Japanese zoni and roasted fish.jpg|thumb|{{nihongo3|||{{ill|Zōni|en|Zōni|qid=Q249169|lt=Zōni|1=Zōni}}}}, a soup with {{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|qid=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}} and grilled fish]]
The Japanese eat a selection of dishes during the New Year celebration called {{nihongo3|||{{ill|Osechi|en|Osechi|qid=Q862557|lt=osechi-ryōri|1=Osechi}}}}, typically shortened to ''osechi.'' Many of these dishes are sweet, sour, or dried, so they can keep without refrigeration: the culinary traditions date to a time before households had refrigerators and when most stores closed for the holidays. There are many variations of ''osechi'', and some foods eaten in one region are not eaten in other places (or are even considered inauspicious or banned) on New Year's Day. Another popular dish is {{nihongo3|||[[:en:ozōni|ozōni]]}}, a soup with ''{{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|qid=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}}'' rice cake and other ingredients, which differ in various regions of Japan. It is also very common to eat {{ill|Soba|en|Soba|qid=Q753910|lt=buckwheat noodles|1=Soba}} called {{ill|Toshikoshi soba|en|Toshikoshi soba|qid=Q9089179|lt=Toshikoshi soba|1=Toshikoshi soba}} on {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|qid=Q2357104|lt=Ōmisoka|1=Ōmisoka}} ({{ill|New Year's Eve|en|New Year's Eve|qid=Q11269|lt=New Year's Eve|1=New Year's Eve}}). Today, {{ill|Sashimi|en|Sashimi|qid=Q190715|lt=Sashimi|1=Sashimi}} and {{ill|Sushi|en|Sushi|qid=Q46383|lt=Sushi|1=Sushi}} are often eaten, as well as non-Japanese foods. To let the overworked stomach rest, {{nihongo|seven-{{ill|Herb|en|Herb|qid=Q207123|lt=Herb|1=Herb}} rice soup|七草粥|nanakusa-gayu}} is prepared on the seventh day of January, a day known as {{nihongo3|||{{ill|Jinjitsu|en|Jinjitsu|qid=Q13423431|lt=Jinjitsu|1=Jinjitsu}}}}.
==Mochi== [[File:Kagamimochi gorgeous version.jpg|thumb|right|A traditionally ornamented {{nihongo3|||{{ill|Kagami mochi|en|Kagami mochi|qid=Q1432988|lt=Kagami mochi|1=Kagami mochi}}}}]] Another custom is to create and eat {{nihongo|[[:en:Mochi (food)|rice cakes]]||mochi}}. Steamed {{nihongo|[[:en:mochigome|sticky rice]]||mochigome}} is put into a wooden container {{nihongo3|||[[:en:Usu (Mortar)|usu]]}} and patted with water by one person while another person hits it with a large wooden mallet. Mashing the rice, it forms a sticky white {{ill|Dumpling|en|Dumpling|qid=Q1854639|lt=Dumpling|1=Dumpling}}. This is made before New Year's Day and eaten during the beginning of January.
''Mochi'' is made into a New Year's decoration called {{nihongo3|||{{ill|Kagami mochi|en|Kagami mochi|qid=Q1432988|lt=Kagami mochi|1=Kagami mochi}}}}, formed from two round cakes of mochi with a {{nihongo|{{ill|Daidai|en|Daidai|qid=Q3895256|lt=tangerine|1=Daidai}}||daidai}} placed on top. The name ''daidai'' is supposed to be auspicious since it means "several generations."
==Bell ringing== [[File:Joyanokane-groupringsbell-tokyoarea-jan1-2008.ogv|thumb|A group rings a local temple bell shortly after midnight of the New Year (2008)]] At midnight on December 31, {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples all over Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} ring their bells a total of 108 times ({{ill|Joya no Kane|en|Joya no Kane|qid=Q485822|lt=Joya no Kane|1=Joya no Kane}} {{nihongo3||除夜の鐘}}) to symbolize the {{ill|Kleshas (Buddhism)|en|Kleshas (Buddhism)|qid=Q19969527|lt=108 earthly temptations|1=Kleshas (Buddhism)}} in Buddhist belief, and to get rid of the 108 worldly desires regarding sense and feeling in every Japanese citizen. A major attraction is The Watched Night bell, in Tokyo. A traditional Japanese belief is that ringing bells can rid the sins of the passing year. The bell is rung 107 times on the 31st and once past midnight.
==Nenga== [[File:An Instance Of New Year Card In Japan.JPG|thumbnail| {{nihongo3|||Nengajō}}, new year cards in Japan]] The end of December and the beginning of January are the busiest for Japanese post offices. The Japanese have a custom of sending {{nihongo|New Year's Day {{ill|Postcard|en|Postcard|qid=Q192425|lt=Postcard|1=Postcard}}s|年賀状|nengajō}} to their friends and relatives, similar to the Western custom of sending {{ill|Christmas card|en|Christmas card|qid=Q622508|lt=Christmas card|1=Christmas card}}s. The original purpose was to give faraway friends and relatives tidings of oneself and one's immediate family— to tell those whom one did not often meet that he/she was alive and well.
Sending these greetings is timed so they will arrive on January 1. The post office guarantees delivery on that day if the cards are marked with the word ''nengajō'' and mailed between mid-December and a few days before year's end. To deliver them on time, the post office usually hires students part-time.
It is customary to refrain from sending these postcards when there has been a {{ill|Death|en|Death|qid=Q4|lt=Death|1=Death}} in the family during the year. In this case, a family member sends a simple {{nihongo|mourning postcard|喪中葉書|mochū hagaki}} to inform friends and relatives that they should not send New Year's cards, out of respect for the deceased.
People get their ''nengajō'' from many sources. Stationers sell preprinted cards. Most of these have the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|en|Chinese zodiac|qid=Q1976204|lt=Chinese zodiac|1=Chinese zodiac}} sign of the New Year as their design, conventional greetings, or both. The Chinese zodiac has a cycle of 12 years. Each year is represented by an animal. The animals are, in order: {{ill|Rat (zodiac)|en|Rat (zodiac)|qid=Q721997|lt=Rat|1=Rat (zodiac)}}, [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]], {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|en|Tiger (zodiac)|qid=Q740762|lt=Tiger|1=Tiger (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Rabbit (zodiac)|en|Rabbit (zodiac)|qid=Q844723|lt=Rabbit|1=Rabbit (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Dragon (zodiac)|en|Dragon (zodiac)|qid=Q731434|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon (zodiac)}}, [[Snake (zodiac)|Snake]], {{ill|Horse (zodiac)|en|Horse (zodiac)|qid=Q869595|lt=Horse|1=Horse (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Goat (zodiac)|en|Goat (zodiac)|qid=Q867696|lt=Goat|1=Goat (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|en|Monkey (zodiac)|qid=Q740674|lt=Monkey|1=Monkey (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Rooster (zodiac)|en|Rooster (zodiac)|qid=Q822621|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster (zodiac)}}, {{ill|Dog (zodiac)|en|Dog (zodiac)|qid=Q755126|lt=Dog|1=Dog (zodiac)}}, and {{ill|Pig (zodiac)|en|Pig (zodiac)|qid=Q877261|lt=Pig|1=Pig (zodiac)}}. 2020 was the year of the Rat and the most recent start of the cycle. Famous characters like {{ill|Snoopy|en|Snoopy|qid=Q207369|lt=Snoopy|1=Snoopy}}, (2006) and other cartoon characters like {{ill|Mickey Mouse|en|Mickey Mouse|qid=Q11934|lt=Mickey|1=Mickey Mouse}} and {{ill|Minnie Mouse|en|Minnie Mouse|qid=Q11936|lt=Minnie Mouse|1=Minnie Mouse}}, (2008) have been especially popular in their celebrated years.
Addressing is generally done by hand, and is an opportunity to demonstrate one's handwriting (see ''[[:en:Shodō|Shodō]]''). The postcards may have spaces for the sender to write a personal message. Blank cards are available, so people can hand-write or draw their own. Rubber stamps with conventional messages and with the annual animal are for sale at department stores and other outlets, and many people buy [[:en:ink brushes|ink brushes]] for personal greetings. Special printing devices are popular, especially among people who practice crafts. Computer software also lets artists create and print their own designs. Very social individuals, who have hundreds of cards to write, may go to print shops and choose from a wide variety of cards prepared with short messages, so the sender only has to address them. Despite the omnipresence of email, the ''nengajō'' remains very popular, although the younger generation sends hardly any cards. They prefer to exchange digital greetings via mobile phones, and in recent years the wider society gradually has come to accept digital greetings.
Conventional greetings include: *{{nihongo3|' I hope for your favor again in the coming year'|今年もよろしくお願いします|kotoshi mo yoroshiku o-negai-shimasu}} *{{nihongo3|'Happiness to you on the dawn [of a New Year]'|(新年)あけましておめでとうございます|(shinnen) akemashite o-medetō-gozaimasu}} *{{nihongo3|' Happy New Year'|謹賀新年|kinga shinnen}} *{{nihongo3|to celebrate January|賀正|gashō}} *{{nihongo3|' early spring'; in the traditional lunar calendar a year begins in early spring|初春|shoshun/hatsuharu}} *{{nihongo3|to welcome spring|迎春|geishun}}
==''Otoshidama''== [[File:Otoshidama Poti Bag Origami.jpg|thumb|upright=0.5|left|{{nihongo|Pouch|ポチ袋}} made of {{nihongo3||折り紙|origami}}.]] On New Year's Day, Japanese people have a custom known as ''{{ill|otoshidama|ja|お年玉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/otoshidama|1=handsel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2380048}}'' where adult relatives give money to children. It is handed out in small decorated envelopes called ''[[pochibukuro]]'', similar to {{ill|Shūgi-bukuro|en|Shūgi-bukuro|qid=Q3959911|lt=Shūgi-bukuro|1=Shūgi-bukuro}} or Chinese {{ill|red envelope|en|red envelope|qid=Q716009|lt=hóngbāo|1=Red envelope}} and to the Scottish [[:en:handsel|handsel]]. In the Edo period, large stores and wealthy families would give out a small bag of mochi and a {{ill|Mandarin orange|en|Mandarin orange|qid=Q125337|lt=Mandarin orange|1=Mandarin orange}} to spread happiness all around. The amount of money given depends on the age of the child but is usually the same if there is more than one child so that no one feels slighted. It is not uncommon for amounts greater than ¥5,000 (approximately US$50) to be given.
==Poetry== The New Year traditions are also a part of {{ill|Japanese poetry|en|Japanese poetry|qid=Q1683305|lt=Japanese poetry|1=Japanese poetry}}, including {{ill|Haiku|en|Haiku|qid=Q37707|lt=Haiku|1=Haiku}} (poems with 17 syllables, in three lines of five, seven and five) and {{ill|Renga|en|Renga|qid=Q1506883|lt=Renga|1=Renga}} (linked poetry). All of the traditions above would be appropriate to include in haiku as ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|qid=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}'' (season words). There are also haiku that celebrate many of the "first" of the New Year, such as the "first {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}}" (''hatsuhi'') or "first {{ill|Sunrise|en|Sunrise|qid=Q193294|lt=Sunrise|1=Sunrise}}", "first {{ill|laughter|en|laughter|qid=Q170579|lt=laughter|1=Laughter}}" (''waraizome''—starting the New Year with a smile is considered a good sign), and first {{ill|Dream|en|Dream|qid=Q36348|lt=Dream|1=Dream}} (''{{ill|Hatsuyume|en|Hatsuyume|qid=Q1588971|lt=Hatsuyume|1=Hatsuyume}}''). Since the traditional New Year was later in the year than the current date, many of these mention the beginning of spring.
Along with the New Year's Day postcard, haiku might mention "first letter" (''hatsudayori''—meaning the first exchange of letters), "first {{ill|Calligraphy|en|Calligraphy|qid=Q12681|lt=Calligraphy|1=Calligraphy}}" (''{{ill|kakizome|en|kakizome|qid=Q600698|lt=kakizome|1=Kakizome}}''), and "first brush" (''fude hajime'').
== Takarabune == [[File:Takarabune by Hiroshige.png|thumb|230px|Coloured woodblock print of the ''Takarabune'' by [[Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] {{Main|Takarabune}}
During the first three days of the {{ill|New Year|en|New Year|qid=Q34812|lt=New Year|1=New Year}} the Seven Lucky Gods are said to pilot through the heavens to human ports on the ''{{ill|Takarabune|en|Takarabune|qid=Q48723823|lt=Takarabune|1=Takarabune}}'' or Treasure Ship.<ref name="Chiba">{{Cite book |title=The Seven Lucky Gods of Japan |last=Reiko |first=Chiba |date=1966 |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co |pages=9–10 |oclc=40117755}}</ref> A picture of the ship forms an essential part of traditional {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese people}} New Year celebrations.<ref name="V&A">{{Cite web |url=https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige |title=The Treasure Ship |publisher= [[Victoria and Albert Museum]] |access-date=2018-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180104014057/https://collections.vam.ac.uk/item/O74843/the-treasure-ship-woodblock-print-utagawa-hiroshige|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 January 2018}}</ref>
==Games== [[File:Large kite.JPG|thumbnail|Displayed large kite in new year Japan]] It was also customary to play many New Year's games. These include ''{{ill|Hanetsuki|en|Hanetsuki|qid=Q3275794|lt=Hanetsuki|1=Hanetsuki}}'', ''takoage'' ([[:en:kite flying|kite flying]]), ''koma'' ({{ill|spinning top|en|spinning top|qid=Q23856|lt=spinning top|1=Spinning top}}), ''{{ill|Sugoroku|en|Sugoroku|qid=Q1472906|lt=Sugoroku|1=Sugoroku}}'', ''{{ill|fukuwarai|en|fukuwarai|qid=Q4115814|lt=fukuwarai|1=Fukuwarai}}'' (whereby a blindfolded person places paper parts of a face, such as eyes, eyebrows, a nose and a mouth, on a paper face), and ''{{ill|Karuta|en|Karuta|qid=Q1616724|lt=Karuta|1=Karuta}}'' (Japanese playing cards).
==Entertainment== There are many shows created as the end-of-year, and beginning-of-year entertainment, and some being a special edition of the regular shows. For many decades, it has been customary to watch the TV show ''{{ill|Kōhaku Uta Gassen|en|Kōhaku Uta Gassen|qid=Q257913|lt=Kōhaku Uta Gassen|1=Kōhaku Uta Gassen}}'' aired on {{ill|NHK|en|NHK|qid=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}} on New Year's Eve. The show features two teams, red and white, of popular music artists competing against each other.
== Sport == The final of the {{ill|Emperor's Cup|en|Emperor's Cup|qid=Q609289|lt=Emperor's Cup|1=Emperor's Cup}}, the national {{ill|Association football|en|Association football|qid=Q2736|lt=Association football|1=Association football}} elimination tournament in New Year's Day. The final has taken place on New Year's Day since 1969 and is usually aired on NHK.
[[:en:Mixed Martial Arts|Mixed martial arts in Japan]] organizations such as [[:en:Pride FC|Pride FC]] and {{ill|Dream (mixed martial arts)|en|Dream (mixed martial arts)|qid=Q606349|lt=Dream|1=Dream (mixed martial arts)}} have held events on New Year's Eve and {{ill|Rizin Fighting Federation|en|Rizin Fighting Federation|qid=Q21089381|lt=Rizin Fighting Federation|1=Rizin Fighting Federation}} has held New Year's Eve events since its founding in 2015.
==Beethoven's Ninth== {{ill|Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)|en|Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)|qid=Q11989|lt=Beethoven's Ninth Symphony|1=Symphony No. 9 (Beethoven)}}, with accompanying chorus, is traditionally performed throughout Japan during the New Year's season. In December 2009, for example, there were 55 performances of the symphony by various major orchestras and choirs in Japan.<ref>Brasor, Philip, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fm20101224r1.html Japan makes Beethoven's Ninth No. 1 for the holidays]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 24 December 2010, p. 20, retrieved on 24 December 2010; Uranaka, Taiga, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn19991201a5.html Beethoven concert to fete students' wartime sendoff]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 1 December 1999, retrieved on 24 December 2010.</ref>
The Ninth was introduced to {{ill|Japan during World War I|en|Japan during World War I|qid=Q973422|lt=Japan during World War I|1=Japan during World War I}} by German prisoners held at the {{ill|Bandō prisoner-of-war camp|en|Bandō prisoner-of-war camp|qid=Q324270|lt=Bandō prisoner-of-war camp|1=Bandō prisoner-of-war camp}}.<ref name="ST">{{cite news |title=How World War I made Beethoven's Ninth a Japanese New Year's tradition |url=https://www.seattletimes.com/entertainment/classical-music/how-world-war-i-made-beethovens-ninth-a-japanese-new-year-tradition/ |access-date=21 July 2020 |work=[[The Seattle Times]] |date=30 December 2015}}</ref> Japanese orchestras, notably the {{ill|NHK Symphony Orchestra|en|NHK Symphony Orchestra|qid=Q1329364|lt=NHK Symphony Orchestra|1=NHK Symphony Orchestra}}, began performing the symphony in 1925. During {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Imperial government|1=Empire of Japan}} promoted performances of the symphony, including on New Year's Eve, to encourage allegiance to {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=Japanese nationalism|1=Japanese nationalism}}.{{Citation needed|reason=cited article quotes someone's opinion without presenting facts|date=May 2023}} After the war, orchestras and choruses, undergoing economic hard times during the reconstruction of Japan, promoted performances of the piece around New Years because of the popularity of the music with the public. In the 1960s, performances of the symphony at New Years became more widespread, including participation by local choirs and orchestras, and established the tradition which continues to this day.<ref>Brasor, Philip, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/fm20101224r1.html Japan makes Beethoven's Ninth No. 1 for the holidays]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 24 December 2010, p. 20, retrieved on 24 December 2010.</ref>
==Little New Year==
There used to be also an associated festival of {{Nihongo|'''Little New Year'''||koshōgatsu}}, traditionally celebrating the first full moon of the new year, on the 15th day of the first lunar month (approximately mid-February). This was celebrated around January 15, in various respects.{{Clarify|date=January 2024}} The main events of ''koshōgatsu'' are rites and practices praying for a bountiful harvest; {{nihongo|rice gruel with [[:en:adzuki|adzuki]] beans|小豆粥|azukigayu}} is traditionally eaten in the morning and is involved in the {{ill|Kayu ura|en|Kayu ura|qid=Q11604655|lt=rice gruel divination ceremony|1=Kayu ura}}. New Year decorations are taken down around this date and burnt in the [[Sagichō Fire Festival|Sagichō or Dondoyaki fire]], and some temples hold events, such as {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|qid=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}}.
== Japanese Lunar New Year == {{Main|Lunar New Year}} Some regions of Japan, including {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Amami Islands|en|Amami Islands|qid=Q453510|lt=Amami Islands|1=Amami Islands}} in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}, used to celebrate Lunar New Year on the first day of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] (around the {{ill|March equinox|en|March equinox|qid=Q12889843|lt=first day of spring|1=March equinox}}, in February of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Okinawa Prefecture The historical background of why Japan continues to use the "lunar calendar" {{!}} Reading|url=https://toyokeizai.net/articles/-/263829|website=Toyo Keizai Online|date=2019-02-20|accessdate= 2019-12-16|language=ja}}</ref> Nowadays, it is very rare to celebrate lunar new year as the new year is considered January 1.
== See also == {{Portal|Japan|Holidays}} * {{ill|First sunrise|en|First sunrise|qid=Q11396515|lt=First sunrise|1=First sunrise}} * {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}}, the first Shinto shrine visit of the Japanese New Year * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|qid=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[:en:Customs and etiquette of Japan|Customs and etiquette of Japan]] * [[Japanese festivals]] * [[Japanese calendar]] * {{ill|Japanese cuisine|en|Japanese cuisine|qid=Q234138|lt=Japanese cuisine|1=Japanese cuisine}} * {{ill|Toso|en|Toso|qid=Q3176373|lt=Toso|1=Toso}}, spiced medicinal sake * {{ill|Namahage|en|Namahage|qid=Q1133889|lt=Namahage|1=Namahage}} * Celebrations of Lunar New Year in other parts of Asia: ** {{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|qid=Q131772|lt=Chinese New Year|1=Chinese New Year}} ''(Spring Festival)'' ** {{ill|Korean New Year|en|Korean New Year|qid=Q8249787|lt=Korean New Year|1=Korean New Year}} ''(Seollal)'' ** {{ill|Tsagaan Sar|en|Tsagaan Sar|qid=Q2340640|lt=Mongolian New Year|1=Tsagaan Sar}} ''(Tsagaan Sar)'' ** {{ill|Losar|en|Losar|qid=Q1347838|lt=Tibetan New Year|1=Losar}} ''(Losar)'' ** [[:en:Vietnamese New Year|Vietnamese New Year]] ''(Tết Nguyên Đán)'' *Similar Asian Lunisolar New Year celebrations that occur in April: **{{ill|Thingyan|en|Thingyan|qid=Q2072215|lt=Burmese New Year|1=Thingyan}} ''(Thingyan)'' **{{ill|Cambodian New Year|en|Cambodian New Year|qid=Q2961613|lt=Cambodian New Year|1=Cambodian New Year}} ''(Chaul Chnam Thmey)'' **{{ill|Lao New Year|en|Lao New Year|qid=Q3115786|lt=Lao New Year|1=Lao New Year}} ''(Pii Mai)'' **{{ill|Sinhalese New Year|en|Sinhalese New Year|qid=Q3534698|lt=Sri Lankan New Year|1=Sinhalese New Year}} ''(Aluth Avuruddu)'' **{{ill|Songkran (Thailand)|en|Songkran (Thailand)|qid=Q215014|lt=Thai New Year|1=Songkran (Thailand)}} ''(Songkran)''
